name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَمَا قَدَرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ حَقَّ قَدْرِهِۦٓ إِذْ قَالُوا۟ مَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ بَشَرٍ مِّن شَىْءٍ ۗ قُلْ مَنْ أَنزَلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ ٱلَّذِى جَآءَ بِهِۦ مُوسَىٰ نُورًا وَهُدًى لِّلنَّاسِ ۖ تَجْعَلُونَهُۥ قَرَاطِيسَ تُبْدُونَهَا وَتُخْفُونَ كَثِيرًا ۖ وَعُلِّمْتُم مَّا لَمْ تَعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنتُمْ وَلَآ ءَابَآؤُكُمْ ۖ قُلِ ٱللَّهُ ۖ ثُمَّ ذَرْهُمْ فِى خَوْضِهِمْ يَلْعَبُونَ (٩١)
```

```english
They did not regard Allah with the regard due to Him when they said, ‘Allah has not sent down anything to any human.’ Say, ‘Who had sent down the Book that was brought by Moses as a light and guidance for the people, which you make into parchments that you display, while you conceal much of it, and [by means of which] you were taught what you did not know, [neither] you nor your fathers?’ Say, ‘Allah!’ Then leave them to play around in their impious gossip. (91)
```

```arabic
وَهَـٰذَا كِتَـٰبٌ أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ مُبَارَكٌ مُّصَدِّقُ ٱلَّذِى بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ وَلِتُنذِرَ أُمَّ ٱلْقُرَىٰ وَمَنْ حَوْلَهَا ۚ وَٱلَّذِينَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِهِۦ ۖ وَهُمْ عَلَىٰ صَلَاتِهِمْ يُحَافِظُونَ (٩٢)
```

```english
Blessed is this Book, which We have sent down, confirming what was [revealed] before it, so that you may warn the Mother of Cities and those around it. Those who believe in the Hereafter believe in it, and they are watchful of their prayers. (92)
```

```arabic
وَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنِ ٱفْتَرَىٰ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ كَذِبًا أَوْ قَالَ أُوحِىَ إِلَىَّ وَلَمْ يُوحَ إِلَيْهِ شَىْءٌ وَمَن قَالَ سَأُنزِلُ مِثْلَ مَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ ۗ وَلَوْ تَرَىٰٓ إِذِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ فِى غَمَرَٰتِ ٱلْمَوْتِ وَٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ بَاسِطُوٓا۟ أَيْدِيهِمْ أَخْرِجُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَكُمُ ۖ ٱلْيَوْمَ تُجْزَوْنَ عَذَابَ ٱلْهُونِ بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَقُولُونَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ غَيْرَ ٱلْحَقِّ وَكُنتُمْ عَنْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ تَسْتَكْبِرُونَ (٩٣)
```

```english
Who is a greater wrongdoer than him who fabricates a lie against Allah, or says, ‘It has been revealed to me,’ while nothing was revealed to him, and he who says, ‘I will bring the like of what Allah has sent down?’ Were you to see when the wrongdoers are in the throes of death, and the angels extend their hands [saying]: ‘Give up your souls! Today you shall be requited with a humiliating punishment because of what you used to attribute to Allah untruly, and for your being disdainful towards His signs.’ (93)
```

```arabic
وَلَقَدْ جِئْتُمُونَا فُرَٰدَىٰ كَمَا خَلَقْنَـٰكُمْ أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ وَتَرَكْتُم مَّا خَوَّلْنَـٰكُمْ وَرَآءَ ظُهُورِكُمْ ۖ وَمَا نَرَىٰ مَعَكُمْ شُفَعَآءَكُمُ ٱلَّذِينَ زَعَمْتُمْ أَنَّهُمْ فِيكُمْ شُرَكَـٰٓؤُا۟ ۚ لَقَد تَّقَطَّعَ بَيْنَكُمْ وَضَلَّ عَنكُم مَّا كُنتُمْ تَزْعُمُونَ (٩٤)
```

```english
‘Certainly you have come to Us alone, just as We created you the first time, and left behind whatever We had bestowed on you. We do not see your intercessors with you—those whom you claimed to be [Our] partners in [deciding] you[r] [fate]. Certainly all links between you have been cut, and what you used to claim has forsaken you!’ (94)
```

```arabic
۞ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ فَالِقُ ٱلْحَبِّ وَٱلنَّوَىٰ ۖ يُخْرِجُ ٱلْحَىَّ مِنَ ٱلْمَيِّتِ وَمُخْرِجُ ٱلْمَيِّتِ مِنَ ٱلْحَىِّ ۚ ذَٰلِكُمُ ٱللَّهُ ۖ فَأَنَّىٰ تُؤْفَكُونَ (٩٥)
```

```english
Indeed Allah is the splitter of the grain and the pit. He brings forth the living from the dead and He brings forth the dead from the living. That is Allah! Then where do you stray? (95)
```

```arabic
فَالِقُ ٱلْإِصْبَاحِ وَجَعَلَ ٱلَّيْلَ سَكَنًا وَٱلشَّمْسَ وَٱلْقَمَرَ حُسْبَانًا ۚ ذَٰلِكَ تَقْدِيرُ ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلْعَلِيمِ (٩٦)
```

```english
Splitter of the dawn, He has made the night for rest, and the sun and the moon for calculation. That is the ordaining of the All-mighty, the All-knowing. (96)
```

```arabic
وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى جَعَلَ لَكُمُ ٱلنُّجُومَ لِتَهْتَدُوا۟ بِهَا فِى ظُلُمَـٰتِ ٱلْبَرِّ وَٱلْبَحْرِ ۗ قَدْ فَصَّلْنَا ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لِقَوْمٍ يَعْلَمُونَ (٩٧)
```

```english
It is He who made the stars for you, so that you may be guided by them in the darkness of land and sea. We have certainly elaborated the signs for a people who have knowledge. (97)
```

```arabic
وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنشَأَكُم مِّن نَّفْسٍ وَٰحِدَةٍ فَمُسْتَقَرٌّ وَمُسْتَوْدَعٌ ۗ قَدْ فَصَّلْنَا ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لِقَوْمٍ يَفْقَهُونَ (٩٨)
```

```english
It is He who created you from a single soul, then there is the [enduring] abode and the place of temporary lodging. We have certainly elaborated the signs for a people who understand. (98)
```

```arabic
وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنزَلَ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءً فَأَخْرَجْنَا بِهِۦ نَبَاتَ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ فَأَخْرَجْنَا مِنْهُ خَضِرًا نُّخْرِجُ مِنْهُ حَبًّا مُّتَرَاكِبًا وَمِنَ ٱلنَّخْلِ مِن طَلْعِهَا قِنْوَانٌ دَانِيَةٌ وَجَنَّـٰتٍ مِّنْ أَعْنَابٍ وَٱلزَّيْتُونَ وَٱلرُّمَّانَ مُشْتَبِهًا وَغَيْرَ مُتَشَـٰبِهٍ ۗ ٱنظُرُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ ثَمَرِهِۦٓ إِذَآ أَثْمَرَ وَيَنْعِهِۦٓ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكُمْ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ (٩٩)
```

```english
It is He who sends down water from the sky, and brings forth with it every kind of growing thing. Then from it We bring forth vegetation from which We produce the grain, in clusters, and from the palm-tree, from the spathes of it, low-hanging clusters [of dates], and gardens of grapes, olives and pomegranates, similar and dissimilar. Look at its fruit as it fructifies and ripens. Indeed, there are signs in that for a people who have faith. (99)
```

```arabic
وَجَعَلُوا۟ لِلَّهِ شُرَكَآءَ ٱلْجِنَّ وَخَلَقَهُمْ ۖ وَخَرَقُوا۟ لَهُۥ بَنِينَ وَبَنَـٰتٍۭ بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ ۚ سُبْحَـٰنَهُۥ وَتَعَـٰلَىٰ عَمَّا يَصِفُونَ (١٠٠)
```

```english
They make the jinn partners of Allah, though He has created them, and carve out sons and daughters for Him, without any knowledge. Immaculate is He and exalted above what they allege [concerning Him]! (100)
```

```arabic
بَدِيعُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ أَنَّىٰ يَكُونُ لَهُۥ وَلَدٌ وَلَمْ تَكُن لَّهُۥ صَـٰحِبَةٌ ۖ وَخَلَقَ كُلَّ شَىْءٍ ۖ وَهُوَ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌ (١٠١)
```

```english
The originator of the heavens and the earth—how could He have a child when He has had no spouse? He created all things and He has knowledge of all things. (101)
```

```arabic
ذَٰلِكُمُ ٱللَّهُ رَبُّكُمْ ۖ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۖ خَـٰلِقُ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ فَٱعْبُدُوهُ ۚ وَهُوَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ وَكِيلٌ (١٠٢)
```

```english
That is Allah, your Lord, there is no god except Him, the creator of all things; so worship Him. He watches over all things. (102)
```

```arabic
لَّا تُدْرِكُهُ ٱلْأَبْصَـٰرُ وَهُوَ يُدْرِكُ ٱلْأَبْصَـٰرَ ۖ وَهُوَ ٱللَّطِيفُ ٱلْخَبِيرُ (١٠٣)
```

```english
The sights do not apprehend Him, yet He apprehends the sights, and He is the All-attentive, the All-aware. (103)
```

```arabic
قَدْ جَآءَكُم بَصَآئِرُ مِن رَّبِّكُمْ ۖ فَمَنْ أَبْصَرَ فَلِنَفْسِهِۦ ۖ وَمَنْ عَمِىَ فَعَلَيْهَا ۚ وَمَآ أَنَا۠ عَلَيْكُم بِحَفِيظٍ (١٠٤)
```

```english
[Say,] ‘Certainly insights have come to you from your Lord. So whoever sees, it is to the benefit of his own soul, and whoever remains blind, it is to its detriment, and I am not a keeper over you.’ (104)
```

```arabic
وَكَذَٰلِكَ نُصَرِّفُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ وَلِيَقُولُوا۟ دَرَسْتَ وَلِنُبَيِّنَهُۥ لِقَوْمٍ يَعْلَمُونَ (١٠٥)
```

```english
Thus do We paraphrase the signs variously, lest they should say, ‘You have received instruction,’ and so that We may make it clear for a people who have knowledge. (105)
```

## (Commentary)

The verses are not devoid of a connection to what preceded them; they open with an argumentation (المحاجة) regarding the specific matter of the revelation of the Book to the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب), as they had rejected the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) by their saying: **ما أَنْزَلَ اللهُ عَلى بَشَرٍ مِنْ شَيْءٍ** — "Allah has not sent down anything to any human" (6:91). The preceding verses consider the granting of the Book as one of the necessities of divine guidance (الهداية الإلهية) with which He honored His prophets.

The discourse begins by arguing with the People of the Book, then mentions that the most unjust of all injustices is to associate partners with Allah by fabricating lies against Him, or to act unjustly in the matter of prophethood (النبوة) by denying what is true of it or claiming what is false, such as the one who said: **سَأُنْزِلُ مِثْلَ ما أَنْزَلَ اللهُ** — "I will send down the like of what Allah has sent down" (6:93).

Then the verses mention the ultimate fate of these wrongdoers (الظالمين) during the throes of death (مساءلة الموت) when its agonies (غمراته) envelop them and the angels are stretching out their hands. Subsequently, the text transitions to mentioning the signs of His monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — and mentions some of His Beautiful Names (أسمائه الحسنى) and Sublime Attributes (صفاته العليا).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَما قَدَرُوا اللهَ حَقَّ قَدْرِهِ إِذْ قالُوا ما أَنْزَلَ اللهُ عَلى بَشَرٍ مِنْ شَيْءٍ** — **"They did not regard Allah with the regard due to Him when they said, ‘Allah has not sent down anything to any human’"** (6:91). The word *qadr* (قَدْر) and *qadar* (قَدَر) with the vowel *fatha* refers to the measure (كمية) of a thing in terms of greatness, smallness, or the like. It is said: "I measured (*qadartu*) the thing" (قدرت الشيء قدرا) and "I determined its measure" (*qaddartuhu*) with emphasis (بالتشديد) when you define the quantity and the sensible geometry (هندسته المحسوسة) of a thing. Later, its usage was expanded to non-sensible meanings (المعاني غير المحسوسة); thus, it is said: "So-and-so has a high *qadr* among the people and in society," meaning his greatness in the eyes of the people, his weight in their society, and his social value (قيمته الاجتماعية).

Since determining the measure of a thing and defining it by its limits is usually inseparable from describing it with attributes that clarify its state and lead to its recognition (عرفانه), the terms *qadr* and *taqdir* (تقدير) were applied to description (الوصف) and to the knowledge (المعرفة) of a thing's state—by way of metaphor (استعارة). Thus, it is said: "He measured (*qadara*) the thing," meaning he described it; and "He measured the thing," meaning he knew it. Language permits all these usages.

Since Allah, the Glorified, cannot be encompassed in His transcendent essence (ذاته المتعالية) by sense, imagination, or intellect, and is only known through a certain knowledge (معرفة ما) via the attributes (الأوصاف) befitting His holy presence, and since only that portion of His greatness indicated by His signs and acts can be attained, it is correct to use the term *qadr* regarding Him in all the aforementioned senses. Thus, **ما قَدَرُوا اللهَ حَقَّ قَدْرِهِ** means: they did not magnify Him with the greatness befitting His presence, or they did not describe Him with His true description, or they did not know Him with His true gnosis (معرفته). The verse itself allows for any of these three meanings, or all of them by way of implication (الالتزام). However, the most appropriate meaning—

—considering the preceding verses describing His guidance (هدايته) of His prophets, followed by the granting of the Book, the Judgment, and Prophethood, and His complete care in preserving the word of truth and the blessing of guidance among people time after time and generation after generation—is to take it in the first sense. Indeed, denying the revelation of inspiration (الوحي) is a belittling of His status (قدره) and an exclusion of Him from the station of Lordship (الربوبية) which cares for the affairs of His servants and guides them toward their goal of felicity (السعادة) and success (الفلاح).

This is supported by the similar phrasing in His saying, the Exalted: **وَما قَدَرُوا اللهَ حَقَّ قَدْرِهِ وَالْأَرْضُ جَمِيعاً قَبْضَتُهُ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ وَالسَّماواتُ مَطْوِيَّاتٌ بِيَمِينِهِ سُبْحانَهُ وَتَعالى عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ** — **"They do not regard Allah with the regard due to Him, yet the entire earth will be in His fist on the Day of Resurrection, and the heavens, rolled up, in His right hand. Immaculate is He and exalted above [having] any partners they ascribe [to Him]!"** (39:67).

And His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ تَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ لَنْ يَخْلُقُوا ذُباباً وَلَوِ اجْتَمَعُوا لَهُ وَإِنْ يَسْلُبْهُمُ الذُّبابُ شَيْئاً لا يَسْتَنْقِذُوهُ مِنْهُ ضَعُفَ الطَّالِبُ وَالْمَطْلُوبُ ، ما قَدَرُوا اللهَ حَقَّ قَدْرِهِ إِنَّ اللهَ لَقَوِيٌّ عَزِيزٌ** — **"Indeed those whom you invoke besides Allah will never create a fly even if they rallied together for it. And if a fly should take away something from them, they cannot take it back from it. Feeble is the seeker and the sought! They do not regard Allah with the regard due to Him. Indeed Allah is All-strong, All-mighty."** (22:73-74). That is, His power and might, and the weakness and humiliation of others, necessitate that His status not be belittled, nor should He be equated with those they invoke besides Him by naming them all "gods" and "lords." Thus, the first meaning is most appropriate for the verse, even if the other two meanings are not precluded. As for the interpretation of **ما قَدَرُوا اللهَ حَقَّ قَدْرِهِ** as meaning "they did not attribute to Him the power (القدرة) that is His right," as some have interpreted it, it is the most remote of the possible meanings from the context of the verse.

When His saying, the Exalted, **وَما قَدَرُوا اللهَ حَقَّ قَدْرِهِ** was restricted by the temporal clause (الظرف) in His saying, **إِذْ قالُوا ما أَنْزَلَ اللهُ عَلى بَشَرٍ مِنْ شَيْءٍ**, it indicated that their audacity against Allah, the Glorified, and their failure to regard Him with His due regard, stemmed from their denial that He sends down inspiration (الوحي) and the Book to a human. This proves that it is a necessity of Divinity (الألوهية) and a characteristic of Lordship (الربوبية) to send down inspiration and the Book for the purpose of guiding people to the straight path (الصراط المستقيم) — *the direct and unerring way to Allah* — and attaining the felicity of this world and the Hereafter. This is the claim.

He, the Exalted, pointed to the proof of this claim and the argumentation for it by His saying: **قُلْ مَنْ أَنْزَلَ الْكِتابَ الَّذِي جاءَ بِهِ مُوسى** — **"Say, ‘Who sent down the Book that was brought by Moses...’"** etc., and by His saying: **وَعُلِّمْتُمْ ما لَمْ تَعْلَمُوا أَنْتُمْ وَلا آباؤُكُمْ** — **"and you were taught what you did not know, [neither] you nor your fathers?"** (6:91). The first of these two statements is an argumentation using one of the revealed heavenly books (الكتب السماوية) sent to the prophets (عليه السلام) whose prophethood was established by the manifest miracles (المعجزات) they brought. In this, there is an adherence to the existence of continuous divine guidance (الهداية الإلهية) preserved among people through the prophets (عليه السلام), from Noah and those after him, which Allah described in the preceding verses from His saying: **وَإِذْ قالَ إِبْراهِيمُ لِأَبِيهِ آزَرَ** — **"When Abraham said to Azar, his father..."** (6:74) until His saying: **إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا ذِكْرى لِلْعالَمِينَ** — **"It is nothing but a reminder for all the nations."** (6:90).

The second of the two statements is an argumentation based on the existence of divine gnosis (معارف) and laws (أحكام) among people that are not of the nature to emanate from social man (الإنسان الاجتماعي) by virtue of his society, with its emotions and thoughts that guide him to what rectifies his life in terms of food, shelter, clothing, marriage, seeking benefits, and repelling harms and dislikes. These matters, which are in the course of enjoying material things (الماديات), are what man seeks according to his animal nature (طبعه الحيواني). As for divine gnosis, virtuous and wholesome ethics (الأخلاق الفاضلة), and the religious laws (الشرائع) that preserve both through practice, these are not among the things social man attains through his social consciousness (شعوره الاجتماعي). How could social consciousness achieve that? It only drives man to employ all means possible to reach his goals in earthly life and his objectives in food, drink, marriage, clothing, shelter, and related matters. Then it calls him to break the resistance of whatever opposes him in the path of his enjoyment if he is able, or to reconcile for mutual aid and partnership in benefits and the observance of justice in their distribution if he is not. This is the secret of man being a social and civil being (اجتماعيا مدنيا), as clarified in the discussions on prophethood in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: **كانَ النَّاسُ أُمَّةً واحِدَةً فَبَعَثَ اللهُ النَّبِيِّينَ** — **"Mankind were but one community; then Allah sent the prophets..."** (2:213) in the second volume of this book, and we shall increase its clarity, Allah willing.

In summary, the verse—meaning His saying, the Exalted: **وَما قَدَرُوا اللهَ حَقَّ قَدْرِهِ**—indicates, through its context, that it is a necessity of Divinity to guide man to the straight path and the abode of felicity by sending down the Book and inspiration to some of its individuals. It argues for this, firstly, by the existence of some books sent down by Allah in the path of guidance, and secondly, by the existence of that which indicates divine teachings among them that man cannot attain through his social intellect (العقل الاجتماعي).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قُلْ مَنْ أَنْزَلَ الْكِتابَ الَّذِي جاءَ بِهِ مُوسى نُوراً وَهُدىً لِلنَّاسِ تَجْعَلُونَهُ قَراطِيسَ تُبْدُونَها وَتُخْفُونَ كَثِيراً** — **"Say, ‘Who sent down the Book that was brought by Moses as a light and guidance for the people, which you make into manuscripts, exposing [part of] it and hiding much...’"** (6:91). The common reading is *taj‘alūnahu* (تَجْعَلُونَهُ) in the second-person plural (صيغة الخطاب), and those addressed are inevitably the Jews. It has also been read as *yaj‘alūnahu* (يَجْعَلُونَهُ) in the third-person (صيغة الغيبة), in which case the one addressed and questioned by His saying, **مَنْ أَنْزَلَ الْكِتابَ** etc., is either the Jews or the Arab polytheists, as has been said. The meaning of "making the Book into manuscripts" (قراطيس) — which is the plural of *qirtās* (قرطاس) — is either putting it into manuscripts by writing it therein, or making it the manuscripts themselves including the writing within them; for pages and manuscripts are called a "book" (كتاب) just as the words indicated by the writing are called a "book."

His saying: **قُلْ مَنْ أَنْزَلَ الْكِتابَ الَّذِي جاءَ بِهِ مُوسى** etc., is a response to their saying recounted in His saying, the Exalted: **إِذْ قالُوا ما أَنْزَلَ اللهُ عَلى بَشَرٍ مِنْ شَيْءٍ**. Although the verse does not specify who the speakers of this statement were, the response, with its specific characteristics, leaves no doubt that those addressed by this response are the Jews. Thus, those who said, "Allah has not sent down anything to any human," are also the Jews. This is because the verse argues against these speakers using the Book of Moses (عليه السلام), and the polytheists (المشركون) do not acknowledge it nor do they say it was sent down by Allah; rather, those who believe in it are the People of the Book. Furthermore, the verse censures them for making it into manuscripts, exposing some and hiding much, and this too is among the characteristics of the Jews according to what the Quran attributes to them, rather than the polytheists.

Moreover, His subsequent saying: **وَعُلِّمْتُمْ ما لَمْ تَعْلَمُوا أَنْتُمْ وَلا آباؤُكُمْ** — **"and you were taught what you did not know, [neither] you nor your fathers?"** (6:91), according to its plain and simple meaning, is not suitable to address anyone other than the Jews—neither the polytheists nor the Muslims—as previously mentioned and as will follow, Allah willing.

As for the objection that the Jews believed in the prophethood of the prophets—Moses and those before him (عليه السلام)—and in the revelation of heavenly books like the Torah and others, and thus it would not be possible for them to say, "Allah has not sent down anything to any human," because it contradicts the foundations of their beliefs: this is refuted by the fact that its contradiction of their own principles does not prevent some of them from uttering it out of prejudice (تعصبا) against Islam, or to incite the polytheists against the Muslims, or saying it in response to a question asked by the polytheists regarding the state of a book that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) claimed was sent down to him from Allah, the Glorified. They even said, in support of the idolatry of the Arab polytheists over the monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — of the Muslims: **هؤُلاءِ أَهْدى مِنَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا سَبِيلاً** — **"These are better guided on the way than the believers"** (4:51). Thus, they preferred the filth of polytheism over the purity of monotheism, even though the foundation of their religion is monotheism, until Allah revealed: **أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا نَصِيباً مِنَ الْكِتابِ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِالْجِبْتِ وَالطَّاغُوتِ وَيَقُولُونَ لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا هؤُلاءِ أَهْدى مِنَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا سَبِيلاً** — **"Have you not regarded those who were given a share of the Book, believing in idols and tyrants and saying of the faithless, ‘These are better guided on the way than the believers’?"** (4:51).

Another example of their sayings—which is even more manifest in its folly than the previous one—uttered out of rage against the Christians, was that Abraham (عليه السلام) was a Jew, until His saying, the Exalted, was revealed regarding them: **يا أَهْلَ الْكِتابِ لِمَ تُحَاجُّونَ فِي إِبْراهِيمَ وَما أُنْزِلَتِ التَّوْراةُ وَالْإِنْجِيلُ إِلَّا مِنْ بعده أَفَلا تَعْقِلُونَ** — **"O People of the Book! Why do you argue concerning Abraham, when the Torah and the Evangel were not sent down until after him? Do you not exercise your reason?"** (3:65) until He said: **ما كانَ إِبْراهِيمُ يَهُودِيًّا وَلا نَصْرانِيًّا وَلكِنْ كانَ حَنِيفاً مُسْلِماً وَما كانَ مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ** — **"Abraham was neither a Jew nor a Christian, but he was a Hanif, a Muslim, and he was not one of the polytheists."** (3:67), among other sayings of theirs that contradict their established principles as recounted in the Noble Quran.

One whose state is such, it is not far-fetched that he would deny the revelation of a heavenly book to a human for some corrupt motive that drives him to deny what would harm him if established, or to instill a falsehood in others that he knows to be false in order to benefit from it for some of his invalid purposes.

As for the claim of those who say that the Quran did not concern itself with the matter of the People of the Book in its verses revealed in Mecca, and that the call (الدعوة) in Mecca before the Migration (الهجرة) was directed at the polytheists because of the trial involving their group and because the land was theirs: the response is that this does not necessitate total silence regarding them. The religion is universal and its call encompasses all people; the Quran is a reminder for all the worlds (العالمين), and they and the polytheists were neighbors constantly in contact. The People of the Book have been mentioned in some Meccan surahs without any apparent evidence of the verse being Medinan, such as His saying, the Exalted: **وَلا تُجادِلُوا أَهْلَ الْكِتابِ إِلَّا بِالَّتِي هِيَ أَحْسَنُ إِلَّا الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا مِنْهُمْ** — **"Do not dispute with the People of the Book except in a manner which is best, barring such of them as are wrongdoers"** (29:46), and His saying: **وَعَلَى الَّذِينَ هادُوا حَرَّمْنا ما قَصَصْنا عَلَيْكَ مِنْ قَبْلُ وَما ظَلَمْناهُمْ وَلكِنْ كانُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ يَظْلِمُونَ** — **"We forbade the Jews what We have already related to you, and We did not wrong them, but they used to wrong themselves"** (16:118). Also, many of the injustices of the Children of Israel are mentioned in Surah al-A'raf, despite the surah being Meccan.

It is highly improbable that the Islamic call would continue for years before the Migration while within the Arabian Peninsula there were groups of Jews and Christians, yet news of it would not reach them, or it would reach them and they would remain silent, saying nothing for or against it—especially since a group of Muslims migrated to Abyssinia and recited the Meccan Surah Maryam to them, which contains the story of Jesus and his prophethood.

As for the claim of those who say that the surah—meaning Surah al-An'am—was only revealed to argue with the polytheists regarding the monotheism (التوحيد) of Allah, the Glorified, and that the general addresses within it are directed at them, so there is no justification for referring the pronoun in His saying, **إِذْ قالُوا ما أَنْزَلَ اللهُ عَلى بَشَرٍ مِنْ شَيْءٍ**, to the Jews; rather, it must refer to the Arab polytheists because the discourse is in the context of reporting about them, and the Jews have not been mentioned in this surah. Thus, it is not permissible to divert the verse from what its context necessitates from its beginning to this point, or even to its end, without proof from a sound report (خبر صحيح) or reason (عقل). Therefore, the preferred reading is *yaj‘alūnahu* (يَجْعَلُونَهُ) etc., with the third-person *yā’* (ياء الغيبة), meaning that the Jews make it so; thus, it is a narrative about them mentioned within the address to the Arab polytheists.

Regarding the problem that the polytheists did not believe the Torah to be a heavenly book, so how could they be argued with using it? Some have answered that the polytheists knew the Jews were the people of the Torah revealed to Moses (عليه السلام), so it is possible they were argued with from this perspective.

The response to this is: although the context of the surah in the preceding verses was indeed for arguing with the polytheists, it was not because of them specifically as individuals. The Quranic exposition does not concern itself with a person or persons for their own sake, but rather because they are arrogant toward the truth and deny the foundations of the call, which are Monotheism, Prophethood, and the Resurrection (المعاد). Thus, those who deny these truths or some of them are the ones intended by the argumentations presented therein. What, then, prevents the mention of some of the lapses of the Jews if it involves the denial of prophethood and the revelation of the Book, as it falls within the purpose of the surah? It occurs alongside the lapses of the polytheists in denying the foundations of the divine religion, even if the speaker is someone other than the polytheists and idol-worshippers. Perhaps it was something they instilled in some of the polytheists seeking discord (الفتنة), as it has been reported in some traditions that the polytheists would sometimes ask them about the state of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and would sometimes send delegations to them for that purpose.

Furthermore, His saying: **وَعُلِّمْتُمْ ما لَمْ تَعْلَمُوا أَنْتُمْ وَلا آباؤُكُمْ**, as will follow, cannot correctly address anyone other than the Jews, just as it is not correct to address anyone other than the Jews with His saying, the Exalted: **قُلْ مَنْ أَنْزَلَ الْكِتابَ الَّذِي جاءَ بِهِ مُوسى نُوراً وَهُدىً لِلنَّاسِ**. The claim that the Arab polytheists knew the Jews were the people of the Torah of Moses is certainly not convincing; for knowing that the Jews are the people of the Torah does not validate the argumentation based on the Torah being sent down by Allah, the Glorified—especially when describing it as "a light and guidance for the people." Belief in its revelation from Allah is different from knowing that the Jews claim such, and it is the former that validates the address, not the latter.

As for the reading *yaj‘alūnahu* (يَجْعَلُونَهُ) etc., the proper way is to take it as a shift in perspective (التفات) while maintaining the address in His saying, **مَنْ أَنْزَلَ الْكِتابَ الَّذِي جاءَ بِهِ مُوسى**, and His saying, **وَعُلِّمْتُمْ ما لَمْ تَعْلَمُوا أَنْتُمْ وَلا آباؤُكُمْ**, as being for the Jews.

Some have attempted to repel the objections raised against making the address in the verse for the polytheists while validating both readings, saying in summary: The verse was revealed as part of the surah in Mecca, as read by Ibn Kathir and Abu Amr—*yaj‘alūnahu* (يَجْعَلُونَهُ) in the third person—arguing against the polytheists of Mecca who denied inspiration (الوحي) because they deemed it improbable that Allah would address humans with anything. Yet they acknowledged the Book of Moses and sent a delegation to the Jewish rabbis, conceding that they were the people of the first Book who knew the reports of the prophets.

Thus, He, the Exalted, says to His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): Say to those who did not regard Allah with His due regard when they said, "Allah has not sent down anything to any human," just as they said, "Has Allah sent a human as an apostle?": "Who sent down the Book brought by Moses as a light (*nūran*)" by which the darkness of disbelief and polytheism—which the Children of Israel inherited from the Egyptians—was dispelled, "and a guidance for the people (*hudan li-l-nās*)," meaning those to whom it was sent, through the divine laws and statutes He taught them. They remained upon light and guidance until they differed concerning it and forgot a portion of what they were reminded of; thus, by following their desires, "they make it into manuscripts, exposing [part of] it" that agreed with them, "and hiding much" that did not agree with their desires.

He said: It appears the verse was recited this way in Mecca and likewise in Medina until the Jewish rabbis hid the ruling on stoning and concealed the glad tidings of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and until some of them said, "Allah has not sent down anything to any human," as the polytheists had said before them—if the reports regarding that are sound. At that point, it was not far-fetched nor disruptive to the context for Allah, the Exalted, to instruct His Messenger to recite these sentences in Medina within earshot of the Jews and others using the second-person address for the Jews, saying: **تَجْعَلُونَهُ قَراطِيسَ تُبْدُونَها وَتُخْفُونَ كَثِيراً**, without abrogating the first reading.

He said: With this possibility, supported by the mentioned events, the interpretation of the two readings becomes straightforward without any forced effort, and every difficulty that occurred to the commentators in interpreting them is removed. This is the summary of his words.

You are well aware that the problem of addressing the polytheists with what they do not acknowledge remains as it was, as does the problem of addressing those other than the Jews with His saying: **وَعُلِّمْتُمْ ما لَمْ تَعْلَمُوا أَنْتُمْ وَلا آباؤُكُمْ**, as we have pointed out. Likewise, his restricting His saying, the Exalted, **نُوراً وَهُدىً لِلنَّاسِ**, to the Jews only, and his claim that the Jews said in Medina, "Allah has not sent down anything to any human," returns to what he fled from.

Furthermore, his saying that Allah instructed His Messenger to recite the verse to them and address them with His saying, **تَجْعَلُونَهُ قَراطِيسَ تُبْدُونَها وَتُخْفُونَ كَثِيراً**, is something for which there is no evidence. If he means by this "instruction" a new revelation (وحي) with the second-person address like the first revelation with the third-person, then the verse would have been revealed twice: once as part of the surah as one of its verses, and once in Medina not included in the verses of the surah nor a part of it. If he means by "instruction" something other than revelation through the descent of Gabriel with it, then the verse would not be a verse nor the reading a reading. If it is meant that Allah made His Messenger understand in some way that the phrase **تَجْعَلُونَهُ قَراطِيسَ** etc., revealed to him within Surah al-An'am in Mecca, accommodates both the second-person and third-person addresses, and that both readings are correct and intended—as might be said by those who trace the different readings back to the reading of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) or recitation before him and the like—then this entails all the previous objections, as is evident.

Know that these discussions only arise on the assumption that the verse was revealed in Mecca. As for what occurs in some reports that the verse was revealed in Medina, most of them have no place.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَعُلِّمْتُمْ ما لَمْ تَعْلَمُوا أَنْتُمْ وَلا آباؤُكُمْ** — **"and you were taught what you did not know, [neither] you nor your fathers?"** (6:91). The intended meaning of this knowledge (العلم) which they were taught and which neither they nor their fathers knew is not the ordinary knowledge (العلم العادي) of what is beneficial and harmful in life, for which man is equipped with the means leading to it, such as sense, imagination, and intellect. This is because the discourse occurs in the context of argumentation and is linked to it, and there is no link between man's acquisition of ordinary sciences through the methods placed within him and the claim—which is that it is a necessity of Divinity to guide man to his felicity and to send down inspiration and the Book to some of its individuals.

Nor is the meaning that Allah bestowed upon you the knowledge of things you could not have known by yourselves, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: **وَجعل لَكُمُ السَّمْعَ وَالْأَبْصارَ وَالْأَفْئِدَةَ** — **"and He gave you hearing, eyesight, and hearts"** (16:78) and His saying: **الَّذِي عَلَّمَ بِالْقَلَمِ ، عَلَّمَ الْإِنْسانَ ما لَمْ يَعْلَمْ** — **"Who taught by the pen, taught man what he did not know"** (96:4-5); for the context, as you have seen, contradicts that.

Thus, the meaning of the verse is the teaching of that which is not within the power of man to attain knowledge of through the familiar methods with which he is equipped. This is nothing other than what Allah, the Glorified, revealed to His prophets and the carriers of His inspiration through a book or otherwise, in terms of divine gnosis (المعارف الإلهية), rulings, and religious laws (الشرائع); for these are what the ordinary means available to the generality of mankind cannot attain.

From here it becomes clear that those addressed by this speech—meaning His saying: **وَعُلِّمْتُمْ ما لَمْ تَعْلَمُوا** etc.—are not the polytheists, as they did not possess anything of the gnosis of prophethood and divine laws that they clearly knew and acknowledged. What they had inherited from the remnants of the traces of prophethood from their ancestors was not something they would acknowledge such that it would be correct to argue against them with it without sufficient explanation. Allah has described them with ignorance in verses such as His saying: **وَقالَ الَّذِينَ لا يَعْلَمُونَ لَوْ لا يُكَلِّمُنَا اللهُ** — **"Those who do not know say, ‘Why does not Allah speak to us...’"** (2:118).

So the address is directed to those other than the polytheists. Nor is it directed to the Muslims, firstly: because the context is one of argumentation, and if the address were directed to them, it would be an interruption (اعتراض) in the context of argumentation without any apparent point (نكتة).

Secondly: because of the change in the subject of the address (مورد الخطاب) and the turning away from addressing those spoken to in His saying, **مَنْ أَنْزَلَ الْكِتابَ الَّذِي جاءَ بِهِ مُوسى** etc., to addressing others in His saying, **وَعُلِّمْتُمْ** etc., without an apparent indicator, while causing confusion. Thus, the address is for those other than the polytheists and the Muslims, namely the Jews addressed in the beginning of the verse.

Allah, the Glorified, has argued against the Jews who said, "Allah has not sent down anything to any human," out of stubbornness and seeking discord, through two paths:

The first: the path of contradiction (المناقضة), which is that they believe in the Torah and that it is a book brought by Moses (عليه السلام) as a light and guidance for the people, and their saying, "Allah has not sent down anything to any human," contradicts this. Then He censured them for cutting it into pieces, showing some and hiding much.

The second: that you have learned what was not in your power to learn by yourselves through acquisition (الاكتساب), nor was it in the power of your fathers to know so as to bequeath its knowledge to you. This includes divine gnosis, virtuous and wholesome ethics, and the religious laws and regulations that organize society and balance it in the best manner, cutting off the roots of human social disputes. These, especially the legislative elements among them, are not among the things attained through acquisition. Those that are attained through the path of intellectual acquisition, such as universal divine gnosis regarding Monotheism, Prophethood, the Resurrection, and virtuous ethics in general, are not sufficient by themselves for their establishment in human society. Mere knowledge of something is different from its entry into the stage of action and its establishment at the general social level. The love of enjoying material pleasures and the instinct to employ everything in the path of achieving dominance over the desires of the soul and complete control over what its whims call for, leaves no room for man to search for the treasures of gnosis and truths buried in his primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — and then build and persist upon them in the course of his life. This is especially true if this materialism dominates society and becomes established at its level; it becomes for them a vessel that confines them to material enjoyments, where none of the human virtues can penetrate any of its regions. What remains of the traces of inherited spiritual virtues continues to be forgotten one by one until their society returns to being a simple animal society, as we observe in the "advanced" conditions today: they have plunged into materialism and surrendered to sensory enjoyments, which has occupied every second of their time and diverted them from the Hereafter to the world in a way that has stripped them of engagement with spiritual matters and prevented any thinking about what would make them felicitous in their true, eternal life.

History, in all its recorded reports of nations and creeds, has not noted a single man of politics or government who called to the virtues of human ethics, pure divine gnosis, and the path of godwariness (التقوى) and servitude (العبودية). Rather, the utmost that individual autocratic governments called for was to pave the way for the survival of their authority and the stability of their rule. The goal of social governments—democracies and the like—was to organize the affairs of society according to what the whims of the majority of individuals proposed, whatever that proposal might be, whether virtue or vice, whether it agreed with true intellectual felicity or opposed it. However, if they opposed any of the spiritual virtues, perfections, and high human objectives whose names remained with them and which the primordial nature (الفطرة) forced them to magnify and respect—such as justice, chastity, truthfulness, love of good, sincerity toward the human species, and compassion for the weak—they interpreted them in a way that agreed with their current practice and prevailing custom, as is before our eyes today.

In summary, the social intellect and the material consciousness ruling in societies are not what lead man to this divine gnosis and spiritual virtues, of which human societies, in their diversity and evolution, still contain many names and respect their meanings. Where is the "clinging to the earth" compared to rising above matter and material things?

These are nothing but traces and remnants of the religious call that traces back to the movements of the prophets and their struggles in spreading the word of truth, disseminating the religion of monotheism (التوحيد), and guiding the human species to its true felicity in both its worldly and otherworldly lives. Thus, it traces back to a divine teaching through the path of inspiration (الوحي) and the revelation of heavenly books.

So His saying, the Exalted: **وَعُلِّمْتُمْ ما لَمْ تَعْلَمُوا أَنْتُمْ وَلا آباؤُكُمْ** is an argumentation against the Jews in refuting the saying of the one among them who said: "Allah has not sent down anything to any human," by stating that you possess beneficial knowledge that you did not attain by yourselves, nor did your fathers attain or bequeath it; rather, you only learned it through a path other than this, which is the path of sending down the Book and inspiration from Allah to some humans. Thus, Allah has sent down to some humans that which He taught them, which is the true gnosis and the laws of religion. The Jews possessed much of this kind, which they inherited from their prophets and which the Book of Moses disseminated among them.

It has become clear from what preceded that the meaning of His saying, **وَعُلِّمْتُمْ ما لَمْ تَعْلَمُوا**, is the totality of what traces back from gnosis and laws to inspiration and the Book, not specifically what came in the Book of Moses (عليه السلام), even if what the Jews possessed of it was the gnosis and laws of the Torah—contrary to some commentators. This is because the wording of the verse does not suit such restriction; for it was said: **وَعُلِّمْتُمْ ما لَمْ تَعْلَمُوا** etc., and it did not say "you were taught by it" or "Allah taught you by it."

It was said: **وَعُلِّمْتُمْ** (you were taught) etc., without mentioning the agent (فاعل) of the teaching because that is more appropriate for the context of the deduction. Mentioning the agent in this context would be akin to begging the question (المصادرة بالمطلوب). It is as if it were said: "Among what you possess are sciences that do not trace back to your acquisition or the acquisition of your fathers, so who taught you that?" Then both questions were answered together by His saying: "Allah," exalted is His Name.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قُلِ اللهُ ثُمَّ ذَرْهُمْ فِي خَوْضِهِمْ يَلْعَبُونَ** — **"Say, ‘Allah.’ Then leave them to play in their impious gossip."** (6:91). Since the answer was clear and manifest, admitting no doubt—and an answer of this nature allows the questioner who is arguing to provide it himself without waiting for the one being argued against—Allah commanded His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to undertake the answer himself, saying: **قُلِ اللهُ**, meaning: He who sent down the Book brought by Moses and who taught you what neither you nor your fathers knew is Allah.

Since the claim that Allah has not sent down anything to any human is a form of vain and frivolous speech (لغو القول وهزله) that no one utters except one who is gossiping and playing with truths—especially if the speaker is one of the Jews who acknowledge the Torah of Moses and boast of knowledge and the Book—He commanded him to leave them to their state, saying: **ثُمَّ ذَرْهُمْ فِي خَوْضِهِمْ يَلْعَبُونَ**.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَهذا كِتابٌ أَنْزَلْناهُ مُبارَكٌ مُصَدِّقُ الَّذِي بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ وَلِتُنْذِرَ أُمَّ الْقُرى وَمَنْ حَوْلَها** — **"This is a blessed Book which We have sent down, confirming what was [revealed] before it, so that you may warn the Mother of Cities and those around it."** (6:92). Having pointed out that it is a necessity of Divinity to send down inspiration to a group of humans who are the prophets (عليه السلام), and that there is a true book like the Torah brought by Moses, and other matters taught to humans that trace back only to divine inspiration and unseen teaching, He mentioned that this Quran is also a divine Book sent down from Him in the same way other heavenly books were sent down. The proof for this is its inclusion of that which is the characteristic of a heavenly book sent down from Allah, the Glorified.

From here it becomes clear, firstly: that the purpose in this context relates to the Quran being a Book sent down from Allah, the Exalted, regardless of the one to whom it was sent. Therefore, He said: "a Book We have sent down" (كتاب أنزلناه) and did not say "We have sent it down to you," unlike other instances such as His saying, the Exalted: **كِتابٌ أَنْزَلْناهُ إِلَيْكَ مُبارَكٌ لِيَدَّبَّرُوا آياتِهِ** — **"[It is] a blessed Book that We have sent down to you, so that they may contemplate its signs..."** (38:29) and others.

Secondly: that the attributes mentioned for the Book in His saying, **مُبارَكٌ مُصَدِّقُ** etc., are in the position of proofs for its being sent down from Allah, and they are indeed proofs. Among the signs that it is sent down from Allah is that it is blessed (مبارك); Allah has placed within it blessing (البركة) and much good. It guides people to that which is most upright; through it, Allah guides those who follow His pleasure to the ways of peace. People benefit from it in their worldly life through the gathering of their affairs, the strength of their unity, the oneness of their word, the removal of greed from their souls and grudges from their hearts, the spread of security and peace, the abundance of their sustenance, the goodness of their lives, the clearing of ignorance and every vice from their presence, and their seeking shade under the canopy of their felicity. They also benefit from it in their Hereafter with a great reward and lasting bliss.

If it were not from Allah—whether it were fabricated by a human as a net to deceive people and catch them, or a psychological embellishment, or a satanic suggestion that makes the one who brought it imagine it is a heavenly revelation from Allah when it is not—these divine blessings and much good would not be established within it nor result from it. For the path of evil only leads its traveler to evil, and corruption will never produce rectification. Allah, the Exalted, has said: **فَإِنَّ اللهَ لا يَهْدِي مَنْ يُضِلُّ** — **"Indeed Allah does not guide those who mislead"** (16:37), and He said: **وَاللهُ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الْفاسِقِينَ** — **"and Allah does not guide the transgressing lot"** (61:5), and He said: **وَالْبَلَدُ الطَّيِّبُ يَخْرُجُ نَباتُهُ بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِ وَالَّذِي خَبُثَ لا يَخْرُجُ إِلَّا نَكِداً** — **"As for the good land, its vegetation comes out by the leave of its Lord; and as for that which is bad, it does not yield but scantily."** (7:58).

Among the signs that it is truth is that it confirms what was before it of the true heavenly books sent down from Allah.

Among the signs of that is that it fulfills the divine purpose for His creation, which is to guide them to the felicity of their lives in this world and the Hereafter through warning (الإنذار) by means of the inspiration sent down from Him. This is what is indicated by His saying: **وَلِتُنْذِرَ أُمَّ الْقُرى وَمَنْ حَوْلَها**. The "Mother of Cities" (أُمَّ الْقُرى) is the honored Mecca, and the intended meaning is its people, as evidenced by His saying: **وَمَنْ حَوْلَها** (and those around it). What is meant by "those around it" is all other lands of the earth that it encompasses or that are adjacent to it, as has been said. The speech indicates a divine care for the Mother of Cities, which is the Divine Sanctuary (الحرم الإلهي); from it the call began and the word spread.

From this exposition, it appears that it is most appropriate for the context that His saying, **وَلِتُنْذِرَ أُمَّ الْقُرى**, especially according to the reading *li-yundhira* (لِيُنْذِرَ) in the third person, be coordinated (معطوفا) with His saying, **مُصَدِّقُ**, due to the meaning of purpose (الغاية) it contains. The implied meaning is: "to confirm what was before it and so that you may warn the Mother of Cities," as mentioned by al-Zamakhshari. It has also been said that it is coordinated with His saying, **مُبارَكٌ**, and the implied meaning is: "We sent it down so that you may warn the Mother of Cities and those around it."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَالَّذِينَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِالْآخِرَةِ يُؤْمِنُونَ** etc. — **"Those who believe in the Hereafter believe in it..."** (6:92). It is as if this is a derivation (تفريع) from what Allah, the Glorified, enumerated of the attributes of this Book which He sent down. That is, since this Book which We have sent down is blessed and confirms what was before it, and was sent down for the purpose of warning the people of the earth, those who believe in the Hereafter believe in it because it calls to a lasting otherworldly security and warns them of an eternal punishment.

Then Allah, the Exalted, identified these believers in the Hereafter by one of the most specific attributes of the believers, which is that they maintain their prayer (الصلاة)—which is their worship (العبادة) in which they remember their Lord. This is the attribute with which Allah concluded the attributes of the believers described at the beginning of Surah al-Mu'minun, where He said: **الَّذِينَ هُمْ عَلى صَلَواتِهِمْ يُحافِظُونَ** — **"and who are watchful of their prayers"** (23:9), just as He began with its meaning at its start, saying: **الَّذِينَ هُمْ فِي صَلاتِهِمْ خاشِعُونَ** — **"those who are humble in their prayers"** (23:2).

This is what supports the view that the meaning of "maintenance" (المحافظة) in this verse is humility (الخشوع) in prayer, which is a kind of self-abasement and internal impression from the divine greatness when standing in the station of servitude. However, the well-known interpretation is that maintaining the prayer means maintaining its proper time.

## (A Discussion on the Meaning of Blessing in the Quran)

Al-Raghib mentioned in *al-Mufradat*: The root of *baraka* (بَرَكَ) — with a *fatha* on the *bā’* — is the chest of the camel, though it is used for other things. It is called *birka* (بِرْكَة) — with a *kasra* on the *bā’*. "The camel knelt" (*baraka al-ba‘īr*) means it placed its chest down. From this, the meaning of "adherence" (الملزوم) was derived; thus it is said: "They knelt (*ibtaraku*) in war," meaning they stood firm and adhered to the place of battle. The terms *barākā’* (بَراكاء) and *burūkā’* (بُروكاء) of war refer to the place where the heroes remain firm. "The beast knelt" (*ibtarakat al-dābbah*) means it stood still in a manner like kneeling. The reservoir of water is called a *birka* (بِرْكَة). Blessing (البركة) is the establishment of divine good (الخير الإلهي) in a thing. Allah, the Exalted, said: **لَفَتَحْنا عَلَيْهِمْ بَرَكاتٍ مِنَ السَّماءِ وَالْأَرْضِ** — **"We would have opened for them blessings from the sky and the earth"** (7:96). It is so named because of the establishment of good in it, like the establishment of water in a reservoir. The "blessed" (*al-mubārak*) is that which contains that good; thus: **هذا ذِكْرٌ مُبارَكٌ أَنْزَلْناهُ** — **"This is a blessed Reminder that We have sent down"** (21:50).

He said: Since divine good emanates from where it is not sensed and in a way that cannot be counted or confined, everything in which an imperceptible increase is observed is said to be "blessed" and to contain "blessing." This increase is what is pointed to in the report: "Wealth does not decrease from charity," not to the sensible decrease, as was said by one of the losers when this was mentioned to him and he replied: "Between me and you is the scale." Then he mentioned that the meaning of His being "Blessed" (تباركه تعالى) is His being characterized specifically by goodnesses. (End of quote).

Thus, blessing (البركة) is in reality the good established in a thing and inseparable from it, such as blessing in progeny, which is the abundance of descendants or the eternal preservation of one's memory through them; or blessing in food, such that a great many people are satiated by it, for example; or blessing in time, such that it accommodates an amount of work that its like would not normally accommodate.

However, since religious goals and objectives are restricted to spiritual felicities or the sensory ones that ultimately lead to them, the meaning of blessing occurring in the outward things within religion is spiritual good or that which leads to it. For example, the blessing of Allah, the Exalted, mentioned in the speech of the angels who descended upon Abraham (عليه السلام): **رَحْمَتُ اللهِ وَبَرَكاتُهُ عَلَيْكُمْ أَهْلَ الْبَيْتِ** — **"May Allah’s mercy and His blessings be upon you, O people of the household"** (11:73), refers to various spiritual goodnesses like religion and proximity, and sensory ones like wealth, abundance of progeny, and preservation of memory, all of which are linked to spiritual goodnesses.

Accordingly, blessing—meaning a thing's inclusion of the sought-after good—is like a relative matter that varies according to the objectives. This is because the goodness of a thing is only according to the objective related to it. The objective of food might be to satiate the hungry, or that it does not harm the eater, or that it leads to healing and the rectification of the temperament, or that it becomes a light in the inner self by which man is strengthened for the worship of Allah, and so on. Blessing in it would then be the establishment of any of these goodnesses within it by the success (توفيق) of Allah, the Exalted, between the causes and factors related to it and His removal of obstacles.

From here it becomes clear that the descent of divine blessing upon a thing and the establishment of good within it does not contradict the operation of other factors upon it or the gathering of causes around it. The meaning of Allah's will for a quality or state in a thing is not that He nullifies all other causes and conditions necessitating it—as has been repeatedly mentioned in our previous discussions—for the divine will is a cause in the longitudinal order (طول) of other causes, not in the horizontal order (عرض). His sending down His blessing upon food, for example, is that He brings harmony among the various existing causes so that they do not necessitate in the person a temperamental state by which this food would harm him, and that they do not necessitate its spoilage, loss, theft, or plunder, and so on. It does not mean that Allah nullifies all other causes and undertakes the creation of good within it without their mediation. Understand this.

Blessing is frequently mentioned in the language of religion. It has been mentioned in the Noble Book in many verses with different wordings, and likewise in the Sunnah. Blessing is also repeatedly mentioned in the Two Testaments (العهدين) in many instances where it is mentioned that Allah, the Glorified, gave blessing to such-and-such a prophet, or that the priests gave blessing to others. Receiving blessing in the Old Testament was like a prevailing custom.

What has preceded clarifies the falsity of the claim of those who deny the existence of blessing, as we quoted from al-Raghib in his previous statement. They imagined that the operation of natural causes in things leaves no room for another cause to operate within them or nullify their effect. They failed to realize that His influence, the Exalted, in things is in the longitudinal order of other causes, not in the horizontal order, such that it would lead to conflict or nullification and the like.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنِ افْتَرى عَلَى اللهِ كَذِباً** — **"Who is a greater wrongdoer than him who fabricates a lie against Allah..."** until His saying, **ما أَنْزَلَ اللهُ** — **"what Allah has sent down"** (6:93). Allah, the Glorified, enumerated three instances of wrongdoing (الظلم) that are among its most severe levels, the hideousness and atrocity of which the ordinary intellect does not doubt; therefore, He presented them in the context of a question.

The purpose of this is to call for submission to the judgment of the sound intellect, the adoption of fairness, and the lowering of the wing to the manifest truth. It is as if He is saying: Say to them: It is incumbent upon me and you that we do not act arrogantly toward the truth nor exalt ourselves over Allah, the Exalted, by committing what is among the most severe and hideous of injustices, which is injustice in the presence of Allah. How then can it be correct for you to fabricate a lie against Allah and claim partners for Him whom you take as intercessors? How can it be permissible for me to claim prophethood and say, "It has been revealed to me," if I were not a prophet to whom revelation is sent? And how can it be permissible for a speaker to say, "I will send down the like of what Allah has sent down," thereby mocking the judgment of Allah and ridiculing His signs?

The result of this call is that they should submit to the judgment of prophethood. For if they avoid fabricating lies against Allah through polytheism, and the one who says, "I will send down the like of what Allah has sent down," ceases his speech, while the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) persists in the revelation, his prophethood remains without a challenger.

Fabricating a lie against Allah, the Glorified—which is the first of the enumerated injustices—is more general than the claim of revelation when it has not been revealed to him—which is the second of the enumerated injustices. Therefore, it has been said that mentioning the second after the first is of the category of mentioning the specific after the general out of concern for the status of revelation and to magnify its matter. However, contemplation of the context of the speech and its direction toward the polytheists suggests that the intended meaning of the mentioned fabrication is the taking of a partner for Allah, the Glorified. It was not stated explicitly so as to avoid the problem of mentioning the specific after the general, because the purpose here—as mentioned—is to call for fairness and the avoidance of the prejudice of ignorance (عصبية الجاهلية). Thus, the objective was not stated explicitly but was left somewhat ambiguous so that the vein of prejudice would not be stirred nor the motive of pride awakened.

Thus, His saying: **مِمَّنِ افْتَرى عَلَى اللهِ كَذِباً** and His saying: **أَوْ قالَ أُوحِيَ إِلَيَّ وَلَمْ يُوحَ إِلَيْهِ** are distinct in terms of their intended meaning, even if they appear to be general and specific according to their outward form.

This is supported by what is at the end of the verse regarding the threat of punishment and the questioning about partners and intercessors.

As for the claim that His saying, **أَوْ قالَ أُوحِيَ إِلَيَّ وَلَمْ يُوحَ إِلَيْهِ شَيْءٌ**, was revealed concerning Musaylimah when he claimed prophethood: the context of the verses, as you have seen, does not suit this; rather, its outward meaning is that it refers to himself, even if the speech, setting that aside, is more general.

Furthermore, Surah al-An'am is Meccan, and his claim to prophethood was among the events that occurred after the Migration—except that those [who make this claim] view the verse as Medinan rather than Meccan, and the discussion on this will follow in the upcoming traditional research (البحث الروائي), Allah willing.

As for His saying: **وَمَنْ قالَ سَأُنْزِلُ مِثْلَ ما أَنْزَلَ اللهُ**, its outward meaning is that it is a report of an actual saying, and that there was someone who said, "I will send down the like of what Allah has sent down," and that he only said it in mockery of the Noble Quran when he attributed it to Allah by way of "sending down" and then promised the people its like by "sending down." He did not say, "I will say the like of what Muhammad said," or "I will bring you the like of what he brought you."

Therefore, some commentators mentioned that it is an allusion to the saying of those among the polytheists who said: **لَوْ نَشاءُ لَقُلْنا مِثْلَ هذا إِنْ هذا إِلَّا أَساطِيرُ الْأَوَّلِينَ** — **"If we wish, we can say the like of this; these are nothing but fables of the ancients."** (8:31).

Others said: The verse is an allusion to the saying of Abdullah ibn Sa'd ibn Abi Sarh: "I will send down the like of what Allah has sent down," and that the verse is Medinan. Some said other than that, as will follow in the traditional research. The verse does not clearly apply to any of that, for it contains a promise of a future matter, while their saying, "If we wish, we can say..." is a conditional statement, and the saying of Abdullah—if the report is sound—is a report of a current, ongoing matter.

In any case, His saying: **وَمَنْ قالَ سَأُنْزِلُ مِثْلَ ما أَنْزَلَ اللهُ** recounts a saying uttered by some of the Arab polytheists out of arrogance toward the signs of Allah. The relative pronoun *man* (مَنْ) was repeated here but not in His saying, **أَوْ قالَ أُوحِيَ إِلَيَ** etc., because the enumerated injustices, although three, are from another perspective two categories: the first and second are injustice in the presence of Allah in the form of submission to His side and obedience to His command, while the third is injustice in the form of exalting oneself over Him and arrogance toward His signs.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلَوْ تَرى إِذِ الظَّالِمُونَ فِي غَمَراتِ الْمَوْتِ** — **"Were you to see when the wrongdoers are in the throes of death..."** (6:93) until the end of the verse. The root of *ghamr* (الغمر) is to cover a thing and remove its trace; therefore, the term *ghamra* (غمرة) is applied to abundant water that covers what is beneath it, to absolute ignorance (الجهل المطبق), and to the hardship (الشدة) that surrounds its victim. *Ghamarāt* (الغمرات) are hardships; hence His saying: **فِي غَمَراتِ الْمَوْتِ**. *Hawn* (الهون) and *hawān* (الهوان) mean humiliation (الذلة).

The "stretching out of the hand" (بسط اليد) is clear in its meaning, except that what is intended by it is a metaphorical (كنائي) sense, which varies according to the context. The stretching out of the hand by the wealthy is his generosity with his wealth and his kindness to those who deserve it; the stretching out of the hand by the king is his management of the affairs of his kingdom without anyone challenging him; and the stretching out of the hand by the harsh and severe official over the criminal who has been seized is his punishment and tormenting him with striking, rebuking, and the like.

Thus, the stretching out of the hands by the angels is their commencement of punishing the wrongdoers. The apparent context suggests that what the angels do to these wrongdoers is what is expressed by His saying: **أَخْرِجُوا أَنْفُسَكُمُ الْيَوْمَ تُجْزَوْنَ عَذابَ الْهُونِ** — "Give up your souls! Today you shall be requited with a humiliating punishment" (6:93). These sentences are narrated from the angels, not from the speech of Allah, the Glorified; the implied meaning is: "The angels say to them: 'Give up your souls...'" Thus, they punish them by seizing their spirits with a seizing through which they taste painful torment. This is their punishment at the time of death, before they have transitioned from this world to what lies beyond it, and they shall have further punishment after that, even before the Resurrection is established for them, as indicated by the saying of the Exalted: **وَمِنْ وَرائِهِمْ بَرْزَخٌ إِلى يَوْمِ يُبْعَثُونَ** — "and before them is a barrier until the day they are resurrected" (23:100).

From this, it becomes clear that what is meant by "the day" (اليوم) in His saying: **الْيَوْمَ تُجْزَوْنَ** — "Today you shall be requited" — is the day of death in which they are requited with punishment, which is the intermediate realm (البرزخ) — *the state between death and resurrection*. It also becomes clear that the "wrongdoers" (الظالمين) are those who commit any of the three injustices that Allah, the Glorified, counted as the severest of wrongs: namely, fabricating lies against Allah, falsely claiming prophethood, and mocking the signs of Allah.

This is supported by what Allah mentioned regarding the causes of their punishment from among their sins, which is their saying against Allah other than the truth—as is the case with one who fabricates lies against Allah by attributing a partner to Him, or attributing a legislative ruling or a false revelation to Him—and their acting arrogantly toward the signs of Allah, as is the case with one who used to say: **سَأُنْزِلُ مِثْلَ ما أَنْزَلَ اللهُ** — "I will send down the like of what Allah has sent down" (6:93).

Similarly, His saying: **أَخْرِجُوا أَنْفُسَكُمُ** — "Give up your souls" — is a creative command (أمر تكويني), because death and the taking of the soul are not within human power like life, such that one could be commanded to do so [legislatively]. The Exalted said: **وَأَنَّهُ هُوَ أَماتَ وَأَحْيا** — "and that it is He who brings death and gives life" (53:44). Thus, the command is creative, and the angels are among its causes. The phrase is fashioned as a metaphor by way of metonymy (استعارة بالكناية) and an imaginary metaphor (استعارة تخييلية), as if the human soul (النفس) is something inside the body by which it has life, and by its exit from the body, death occurs. This is because His speech, the Exalted, is apparent in that the soul is not of the same genus as the body, nor of the nature of material, corporeal things; rather, it possesses another nature of existence (سنخ آخر من الوجود) that unites with the body and attaches to it with a type of non-material union and attachment, as previously explained in a scholarly discussion in the first volume of this book, and as will come in appropriate places, if Allah wills. Therefore, the meaning of His saying: **أَخْرِجُوا أَنْفُسَكُمُ** is the severing of the attachment of their souls from their bodies, which is death; and the speech is that of the angels, according to what the context provides.

The meaning is: If only you could see when these aforementioned wrongdoers fall into the hardships and agonies of death, while the angels are engaged in punishing them by the severe and violent seizing of their spirits, informing them that they are entering the world of death, punished therein with a punishment of humiliation and abasement as a recompense for their saying against Allah other than the truth and for their arrogance toward His signs.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلَقَدْ جِئْتُمُونا فُرادى كَما خَلَقْناكُمْ أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ** — "Certainly you have come to Us alone, just as We created you the first time" (6:94) to the end of the verse. "Alone" (الفرادى) is the plural of "individual" (فرد), which is that which has become separated from being mixed with another in any form of mixing. Its opposite is "pair" (الزوج), which is that which is mixed with another in some way. Close to these two in meaning are "odd" (الوتر) and "even" (الشفع); the "odd" is that which is not joined to another, and the "even" is that which is joined to another. "Bestowal" (التخويل) is the giving of "possessions" (الخول), meaning wealth and the like, which a person manages through administration and disposal.

What is meant by "intercessors" (الشفعاء) are the lords worshipped besides Allah so that they might be intercessors before Allah; thus, they became, by that, partners to Allah, the Glorified, in His creation. The verse informs of the reality of human life that will manifest to him when he arrives before his Lord upon the taking of his soul, for he shall then witness the reality of his own affair—that he is governed by Divine governance (التدبير الإلهي) and nothing else.

This is just as it was the first time creation brought him into being; and that the claims that were attached to his life—of seeking abundance through means (الأسباب), and seeking support and victory through wealth, children, spouses, clans, and groups, as well as seeking intercession through lords besides Allah leading to polytheism (الإشراك)—all of these are false claims and thoughts that have no effect in the realm of creation (التكوين) at all.

For the human being is a part of the parts of the cosmos (الكون), situated under Divine governance, directed toward the goal that Allah, the Glorified, has set for him, like all other parts of the cosmos. No thing has any sovereignty in Divine governance and direction, except that they are means (أسباب) and causes (علل) whose effect terminates in Him, the Exalted, without them being independent in any part of that effect.

However, when the hand of creation fashioned the human being and brought him into existence, and his gaze fell upon the adornment of life and the apparent means and intercessors, and the pleasures of life attracted him, his soul became attached to them. This called him to cling to the hem of these means and submit to them, and it distracted him from turning his face toward the Causer of causes (مسبب الأسباب) and their Originator (فاطرها), to Whom belongs the entire affair. Thus, he granted these means independence in causality (السببية), having no concern but to attain the pleasures of this material life by submitting to the means. He spent his entire worldly life playing with these claims and illusions into which his soul, distracted by material pleasures, had cast him. His life was consumed by playing with falsehood and being distracted by it from the Truth, as the Exalted said: **وَما هذِهِ الْحَياةُ الدُّنْيا إِلَّا لَهْوٌ وَلَعِبٌ** — "The life of this world is nothing but diversion and play" (29:64).

This is what the Quranic teaching leads to, as it mentions that when a human being exits the garment of servitude (العبودية), he forgets his Lord, which leads him to forget himself. The Exalted said: **نَسُوا اللهَ فَأَنْساهُمْ أَنْفُسَهُمْ أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْفاسِقُونَ** — "They forgot Allah, so He made them forget their own souls. It is they who are the transgressors" (59:19).

But when the soul departs the body upon the arrival of death, its connection to all the material means, causes, and precursors—to which it was linked through the body and connected in this worldly station—is severed. At that moment, he witnesses the futility of their independence and the vanishing of their perceived greatness and influence. The eye of his insight then falls upon the fact that his affair, first and last, is with his Lord and none else, and that he has no Lord besides Him, nor any influencer in his state other than Him.

Thus, His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَقَدْ جِئْتُمُونا فُرادى كَما خَلَقْناكُمْ أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ** — "Certainly you have come to Us alone, just as We created you the first time" — is an allusion to the reality of the matter. His saying: **وَتَرَكْتُمْ ما خَوَّلْناكُمْ وَراءَ ظُهُورِكُمْ** — "and you have left behind your backs whatever We had bestowed on you" — is an exposition of the futility of the means that distracted him from his Lord, which intervened between his initial creation and the day he is taken back to his Lord. His saying: **لَقَدْ تَقَطَّعَ بَيْنَكُمْ وَضَلَّ عَنْكُمْ ما كُنْتُمْ تَزْعُمُونَ** — "The tie between you has been severed, and what you used to assert has forsaken you" — is an exposition of the cause of his separation from the means and their fall from independence and influence; and that the reason for this is the uncovering of the futility of the claims with which the human being was playing throughout his worldly life.

It thus becomes clear that these means and attachments have no share in the human being except as illusions and claims with which he distracts himself and plays.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّ اللهَ فالِقُ الْحَبِّ وَالنَّوى** — "Indeed Allah is the splitter of the grain and the pit" (6:95) to the end of the verse. "Splitting" (الفلق) is cleaving. When the discourse in the previous verse concluded with the negation of the independence of means in their influence, and the futility of their lords being intercessors besides Allah—which led to them being partners to Allah—the discourse turned to explaining that these things by which the human being is distracted from his Lord are nothing but creatures of Allah, governed by His governance. They do not produce any effect nor perform any act in rectifying human life or driving it toward the goals of its creation except by a decree (تقدير) from Allah and a governance that He, and none else, directs. Thus, He, the Exalted, is the Lord, and none other.

For Allah, the Glorified, is He who splits the grain and the pit, so that plants and trees grow from them, from whose grain and fruit people receive sustenance. He is the one who brings forth the living from the dead and the dead from the living—the explanation of this has already passed in the commentary on verse 27 of Surah Al Imran—"That is Allah" (ذلِكم الله) and none else; "so where are you being detoured?" (فأنى تؤفكون), and until when will you be turned away from the Truth to falsehood?

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فالِقُ الْإِصْباحِ وَجَعَلَ اللَّيْلَ سَكَناً** — "The splitter of the dawn, He has made the night for rest" (6:96) to the end of the verse. "Dawn" (الإصباح) with the *kasrah* on the *hamzah* is the morning, and it is originally a verbal noun. "Rest" (السكن) is that in which one finds repose. "Calculation" (الحسبان) is the plural of "account" (حساب), and it is said to be a verbal noun of "he calculated" (حسب حسابا وحسبانا). His saying: **وَجَعَلَ اللَّيْلَ سَكَناً** — "and He made the night for rest" — is conjoined to His saying: **فالِقُ الْإِصْباحِ** — "the splitter of the dawn"; there is no harm in conjoining a verbal sentence to a nominal one if it contains the meaning of an action. It has also been recited as "and the Maker" (وجاعل).

In the splitting of the dawn and the making of the night as a rest—wherein moving things find repose from their movements to renew their strength and repel the fatigue, exhaustion, and weariness that befell them due to their movements throughout the day—and in making the sun and the moon (through the appearance of night, day, months, and years from their movements in the apparent senses) a "calculation" (حسبانا), there is a wondrous decree (تقدير) for movements in this changing and transforming station. Through this, the system of human livelihood is organized and the affair of his life is set straight. Therefore, He concluded it with His saying: **ذلِكَ تَقْدِيرُ الْعَزِيزِ الْعَلِيمِ** — "That is the ordaining of the All-mighty, the All-knowing." For He is the All-mighty (العزيز) whom no overcomer can overpower to spoil any of His governance, and the All-knowing (العليم) who is not ignorant of any of the interests of His kingdom, such that He might organize it in a way that might spoil itself or not endure by its nature.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَهُوَ الَّذِي جَعَلَ لَكُمُ النُّجُومَ لِتَهْتَدُوا بِها** — "It is He who made the stars for you, so that you may be guided by them" (6:97) to the end of the verse. The meaning is clear, and what is meant by "detailing the signs" (تفصيل الآيات) is either detailing them according to the creative making (الجعل التكويني) or detailing them according to the verbal exposition.

There is no contradiction between intending the interests of the human being in his life and livelihood in this station—from what appears to the outward senses of the movements of these great celestial bodies and the gravitating heavenly spheres—and each of these bodies being intended by a separate Divine will and created by a volition (مشية) that pertains to its own essence and specifies its individual person. For the aspects are different, and the realization of some of these aspects does not prevent the realization of others; connection and attachment (الارتباط والاتصال) govern all parts of the world.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَهُوَ الَّذِي أَنْشَأَكُمْ مِنْ نَفْسٍ واحِدَةٍ فَمُسْتَقَرٌّ وَمُسْتَوْدَعٌ** — "It is He who produced you from a single soul, then there is a residing place and a depository" (6:98) to the end of the verse. It has been recited as "residing place" (مُسْتَقَرٌّ) with a *fathah* on the *qaf* and with a *kasrah*. According to the first recitation, it is a noun of place meaning the location of stability; thus, "depository" (مُسْتَوْدَعٌ) would also be a noun of place meaning the location of depositing, which is the place where a trust is put. The mention of the "residing place" and the "depository" occurred in His saying, the Exalted: **وَما مِنْ دَابَّةٍ فِي الْأَرْضِ إِلَّا عَلَى اللهِ رِزْقُها وَيَعْلَمُ مُسْتَقَرَّها وَمُسْتَوْدَعَها كُلٌّ فِي كِتابٍ مُبِينٍ** — "There is no animal on the earth, but that its sustenance is upon Allah, and He knows its residing place and its depository. Everything is in a manifest Book" (11:6). In the speech, there is omission and brevity; the implied meaning is: "Among you is he who is in a residing place, and among you is he who is in a depository." According to the second recitation, which is more weightier, "residing" (مُسْتَقِرٌّ) is an active participle, and "depository" (مُسْتَوْدَعٌ) must be a passive participle; the implied meaning is: "Among you is one who is settled, and among you is one who is deposited and has not yet settled."

It is apparent that what is meant by His saying: **وَهُوَ الَّذِي أَنْشَأَكُمْ مِنْ نَفْسٍ واحِدَةٍ** — "It is He who produced you from a single soul" — is the termination of the human progeny, despite its multiplicity and spread, at Adam, whom the Noble Quran considers the origin of the existing human race. What is meant by the "residing place" (المستقر) is that portion of human individuals who have undergone birth and thus settled on the earth, which is the residing place for this species, as the Exalted said: **وَلَكُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ مُسْتَقَرٌّ** — "and for you there is a residing place on the earth" (2:36). What is meant by the "depository" (المستودع) is whoever is deposited in the loins and wombs and has not yet been born, but will be born after a time. This is what is appropriate for the station of explaining the sign by the production of all individuals of the species from a single individual. It is also possible to take "residing place" and "depository" as mim-infinitives (مصدر ميمي).

He expressed this with the word "production" (الإنشاء) rather than "creation" (الخلق) and the like, which is apparent in instantaneous occurrence and what is like it, rather than gradual process. This meaning is also supported by what was previously mentioned in His saying: **وَما مِنْ دَابَّةٍ فِي الْأَرْضِ إِلَّا عَلَى اللهِ رِزْقُها وَيَعْلَمُ مُسْتَقَرَّها وَمُسْتَوْدَعَها** — as is not hidden—meaning He knows what has settled of them on the earth by the actuality of formation, and what is on the way to formation from that which has not yet actually formed nor settled on the earth.

The meaning is: He is the one who brought you into existence, O assembly of humans, from a single soul, and populated the earth with you until a time; so it remains occupied by you as long as you do not become extinct. Thus, some of you are always settled in it, and some of you are deposited in the loins and wombs, or in the loins only, on the way to settling in it.

The commentators have offered other meanings for the verse, such as the saying of some: that what is meant by producing them from a single soul is creating them from one type of soul, which is the human soul; or that what is meant is the production from one type of psychological and physical composition, which is the human reality composed of a human soul and body.
